entry.title: Mastra npm Takeover, CVE-2026-48907 Exploitation, and Crypto Hijacker Campaigns
entry.content:
This digest window is dominated by software supply chain compromise, active exploitation of internet-facing web components, and financially motivated campaigns abusing trust signals and weak access controls. Reporting also highlights operational disruption across major collaboration and productivity platforms, alongside ongoing concerns about exposure prioritization and public-sector cyber capacity. Mend reported a compromise of more than 140 packages in the @mastra npm scope, where an attacker republished packages with a new dependency on easy-day-js. Version 1.11.22 of easy-day-js introduced an obfuscated postinstall dropper that retrieved a second-stage Node.js payload from 23.254.164.92:8000 and launched it as a detached hidden process communicating with 23.254.164.123:443. The second stage was described as harvesting environment secrets and cryptocurrency wallet data, with affected installations treated in the reporting as fully compromised. CISA ordered U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to address CVE-2026-48907, a maximum-severity improper access control flaw in the Widget Factory Joomla Content Editor plugin. The JCE security team said the vulnerability allows unauthenticated PHP upload and execution, is actively exploited, has public exploit code available, and is being targeted through automated attacks. The flaw was fixed in JCE Pro 2.9.99.6, while the vendor noted that updating does not remediate sites already compromised before patching. Broader exposure data from Intruder underscored why these classes of issues remain operationally significant. Across 3,000 attack surfaces, 60% of organizations had at least one HTTP panel exposed, 42% had an internet-reachable database, and 30% exposed files or information that should not have been public. MySQL, Postgres, API documentation, WordPress admin panels, and RDP were among the most common findings. Check Point Research analyzed a Rust-based cryptocurrency clipboard hijacker campaign disguised as Solana and Pump.fun sniper bots, Aviator Predictor tools, and crash-game predictors. The actor used a WordPress phishing site as a landing hub and promoted payloads through GitHub, SourceForge, YouTube, crypto forums, and posts on legitimate news websites. The campaign also inflated trust signals using coordinated or fake GitHub engagement, SourceForge ratings and downloads, YouTube activity, and benign VirusTotal votes and comments. The Windows payload persisted by copying itself to %APPDATA%\\silke\\silke.exe and creating a Startup folder shortcut, while the macOS variant used an unlocker.command script to remove quarantine attributes and installed a LaunchAgent plist with RunAtLoad and KeepAlive. Both monitored clipboard or pasteboard contents and replaced cryptocurrency wallet addresses with attacker-controlled addresses. IRONSCALES reported a French-language invoice notification sent through Mailgun from an Ecocert accounting subdomain that passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC authentication. The email linked to an Upflow billing portal URL containing a JWT with portal.view permissions, a customer identifier, and a 60-day expiration, allowing anyone with the tokenized link to access the portal without credentials. The invoice requested a 380 euro payment and included French IBAN and BIC details, while a tracking pixel confirmed mailbox activity. BleepingComputer reported that India restricted Telegram access nationwide until June 22 after the National Testing Agency said the platform was used to sell access to leaked exam materials. The disruption extended beyond India, including to users in the UAE, after AS18101 announced Telegram prefixes and the route leaked globally via FLAG Telecom. Researchers confirmed the route hijack but said they found no evidence that the wider disruption was deliberate, while civil society groups criticized the block as disproportionate. Microsoft confirmed it is investigating Office launch issues after Windows updates released on or after June 9, 2026. The issue affects third-party applications using OLE automation to open Office apps or documents, including Word, Excel, PowerPoint, and Access, and may fail without an error message. User reports named affected applications such as CCH Engagement, Zotero, Workpaper Manager, Dentrix, and Softdent. Operationally, The Record covered Sen. Mark Warner’s letter warning of CISA staffing cuts, short-staffed regional divisions, and the disbanding of the MS-ISAC program supporting state and local critical infrastructure operators. Separately, Netskope introduced its Catalyst MSP/SP Program for managed SSE and SASE delivery, while a Hacker News article framed adversarial exposure validation as a way to distinguish theoretical exposure from reachable and consequential risk within Continuous Threat Exposure Management.

Mastra npm Scope Takeover: 140+ Packages Compromised via easy-day-js Dropper — Must Read

An attacker compromised the @mastra npm scope by republishing 140+ packages with a malicious easy-day-js dependency that runs a postinstall dropper to steal secrets and crypto wallets. Any machine or CI system that installed affected packages should be treated as fully compromised, cleaned for persistence, and have credentials rotated.

  • An attacker republished more than 140 packages in the @mastra npm scope with a single added dependency, easy-day-js, and the malicious versions were observed on 2026-06-17.
  • The easy-day-js package version 1.11.22 added an obfuscated postinstall dropper, while version 1.11.21 was a clean prior release with no install hook.
  • Installing an affected @mastra package resolves easy-day-js ^1.11.21 to the malicious 1.11.22 version, causing the postinstall script to run automatically during installation.
  • The dropper downloads a second-stage Node.js payload from 23.254.164.92:8000/update/49890878 and launches it as a detached, hidden process that communicates with 23.254.164.123:443.
  • The second stage harvests environment secrets and cryptocurrency wallet data, and the article says any machine that installed an affected package should be treated as fully compromised.

CISA orders feds to patch max severity Joomla plugin flaw by Friday — Must Read

CISA has ordered U.S. federal agencies to patch an actively exploited maximum-severity Joomla Content Editor plugin flaw, CVE-2026-48907, by Friday. The bug allows unauthenticated attackers to upload and execute PHP code, and sites compromised before patching must be cleaned manually.

  • CISA ordered U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to secure systems affected by CVE-2026-48907 by Friday under Binding Operational Directive 26-04.
  • CVE-2026-48907 is a maximum-severity improper access control vulnerability in the Widget Factory Joomla Content Editor plugin that can allow unauthenticated attackers to upload and execute PHP code.
  • The JCE security team fixed the vulnerability in JCE Pro 2.9.99.6 and warned that it is actively exploited, public exploit code is available, and attacks are automated.
  • The JCE security team said updating closes the entry point but does not clean sites that were compromised before patching.
  • Users of compromised sites are advised to back up rogue profiles, update to JCE 2.9.99.6 or later, delete the attacker’s profile, change all passwords, and run a full server-side malware scan.

From Stars to Upvotes: Fake Reputation Fueling a Crypto Clipboard Hijacker — Must Read

Check Point researchers found a Rust-based crypto clipboard hijacker distributed through fake “trading” and gambling tools, with the operator using inflated GitHub/SourceForge engagement, AI YouTube videos, news-site posts, and VirusTotal upvotes to make the malware look legitimate. The campaign targets Windows and macOS users and replaces copied cryptocurrency wallet addresses with attacker-controlled ones to steal funds.

  • Check Point Research analyzed a campaign distributing Rust-based cryptocurrency clipboard hijackers disguised as tools such as Solana and Pump.fun sniper bots, Aviator Predictor, and crash-game predictors.
  • The threat actor used a WordPress phishing site as a central landing page and promoted the malicious tools through GitHub, SourceForge, YouTube, crypto forums, and posts on legitimate news websites.
  • The campaign inflated trust signals with fake or coordinated engagement, including GitHub stars and forks, SourceForge ratings and downloads, YouTube views and comments, and benign VirusTotal votes and comments.
  • The Windows payload persists by copying itself to %APPDATA%\\silke\\silke.exe and creating a Startup folder shortcut, then monitors the clipboard and replaces cryptocurrency wallet addresses with attacker-controlled addresses.
  • The macOS payload uses an “unlocker.command” script to remove quarantine attributes and installs a LaunchAgent plist with RunAtLoad and KeepAlive to maintain persistence while monitoring and replacing wallet addresses in the pasteboard.

India's Telegram ban hit the UAE too. Here's how to get around it — Should Read

India temporarily banned Telegram over alleged NEET exam-leak activity, and a misconfigured BGP route tied to Reliance Communications appears to have disrupted Telegram access beyond India, including in the UAE. Affected users can restore access with Telegram’s built-in MTProto proxy feature, though proxy operators may still see connection metadata.

  • India’s Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology restricted access to Telegram nationwide until June 22 under Section 69A of the IT Act after the National Testing Agency said Telegram was used to sell access to leaked exam materials.
  • Telegram CEO Pavel Durov alleged that Reliance was sabotaging Telegram access outside India through BGP hijacking, while network researchers confirmed AS18101 hijacked Telegram routes but said there was no evidence the disruption was deliberate.
  • The routing leak affected users outside India, including in the UAE, after AS18101 announced Telegram prefixes and the route leaked to the global internet via FLAG Telecom.
  • The Internet Freedom Foundation said the nationwide Telegram block was disproportionate and constitutionally incompatible because it affected more than 150 million users to address fraud committed by a smaller group.
  • The article says affected users can restore Telegram access by configuring the app’s built-in MTProto proxy feature with a trusted proxy server, port, and secret.

The Invoice Portal Link That Didn't Need a Password — May Read

A fully authenticated invoice email from a legitimate Ecocert accounting subdomain used a tokenized Upflow billing portal link that granted passwordless access via a long-lived JWT. The key risk is that valid authentication and clean URLs can still expose billing portals and enable invoice fraud when access tokens are embedded directly in emailed links.

  • A French-language invoice notification was sent from comptabilitegreenlife@accounting.ecocert.com through Mailgun and passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC authentication with compauth=100.
  • The email linked to an Upflow customer billing portal through a redirect on upflow-email.accounting.ecocert.com, and the destination URL contained a JWT with portal.view permissions, a customer identifier, and a 60-day expiration.
  • Anyone possessing the tokenized URL could access the billing portal without entering credentials.
  • The attached PDF invoice, Facture-FR03IN26001665.pdf, requested a 380 euro payment and included French IBAN and BIC payment details.
  • A 1x1 tracking pixel from the upflow-email subdomain confirmed mailbox activity, and Themis quarantined four affected mailboxes after flagging the invoice phishing pattern at 71% confidence.

Microsoft confirms Office apps launch issues after June updates — May Read

Microsoft says Windows updates released on or after June 9, 2026 can prevent some third-party apps using OLE automation from launching Office apps or opening Office documents. There is no fix yet, but users can work around it by opening Office apps or documents directly while Microsoft prepares a future Windows update.

  • Microsoft is investigating an issue where certain third-party applications cannot launch Microsoft Office applications or open documents after installing Windows updates released on or after June 9, 2026.
  • The issue affects Office applications including Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Access, and other Microsoft Office apps when launched from impacted third-party applications.
  • Microsoft said the problem affects certain third-party applications that use OLE automation to interact with Microsoft Office applications, and the Office app or document may fail to open without an error message.
  • User reports identify affected third-party applications including CCH Engagement, Zotero, Workpaper Manager, Dentrix, Softdent, and similar applications.
  • Microsoft advised affected users to open Office applications or documents directly as a workaround while it works on a resolution for a future Windows update.

Warner warns of CISA cuts, staffing gaps in letter to acting chief — May Read

Sen. Mark Warner warned CISA’s acting director that major staff cuts, leadership vacancies, regional staffing gaps and loss of MS-ISAC funding are undermining the agency’s ability to support state and local critical infrastructure cybersecurity. He is pressing DHS and CISA for staffing, service-delivery and hiring details while urging renewed funding for the MS-ISAC.

  • Sen. Mark Warner sent CISA Acting Director Nick Andersen a letter expressing alarm over widespread agency cuts, short-staffed regional divisions, and the disbanding of the MS-ISAC program supporting state and local critical infrastructure operators.
  • The article says the administration has cut about one-third of CISA’s staff and proposed more than $700 million in cuts to CISA’s fiscal year 2027 budget.
  • Warner said state and local officials, industry leaders, law enforcement, education advocates, and cybersecurity experts have reported reduced responsiveness and support from CISA amid staffing turbulence.
  • CISA Acting Director Nick Andersen recently announced that CISA is hiring more than 300 additional workers, with some already having started.
  • Warner asked CISA to provide organizational charts, vacancy explanations, regional service metrics, and details on how many new hires will be assigned to regional jobs and what experience they have.

The Top 10 Attack Surface Exposures in 2026 — May Read

Intruder’s analysis of 3,000 attack surfaces found that many organizations are exposing unnecessary internet-facing assets, including databases, admin panels, API docs, and legacy services. The key takeaway is that reducing what is publicly reachable is as important as patching, especially as exploitation timelines shrink.

  • Intruder analyzed 3,000 attack surfaces and found that 60% of organizations had at least one HTTP panel exposed.
  • Nearly half of organizations had a risky port or service exposed, 42% had a database reachable directly from the internet, and 30% had files or information publicly accessible that should not have been.
  • The most common exposure was MySQL databases at 26%, followed by Postgres databases at 16%, API documentation at 15%, and WordPress admin panels at 15%.
  • API documentation ranked third among exposures, ahead of Remote Desktop Service, which ranked fifth at 11%.
  • The article states that services such as SNMP, UPnP, NTP, and RPC were designed for internal networks and were never meant to be internet-facing.

Built for MSP SASE Growth: Introducing Netskope Catalyst Program — Optional

Netskope introduced its Catalyst MSP/SP Program to help managed service providers onboard SASE customers faster, manage multi-tenant environments, and improve margins. The program includes tiered benefits, self-service tenant provisioning, training, support, pricing incentives, and go-to-market resources.

  • Netskope introduced the Catalyst MSP/SP Program to help service providers run managed SSE and SASE businesses across multiple customers at scale.
  • The program has three partner tiers, Foundation, Ascent, and Summit, which unlock progressively greater benefits across six pillars.
  • Netskope Partner Orchestrator allows partners to create new customer tenants in under 15 minutes through a self-service portal and provides license portability and granular multi-tenant access controls.
  • Catalyst includes tiered programmatic discounts, no upfront financial commitments for managed offerings, and 24×7 MSP support without required add-ons.
  • The program offers expanded training and accreditations, Market Development Funds, co-branded demand generation programs, service creation workshops, a unified partner portal, and deal protection for registered opportunities.

Adversarial Exposure Validation Turns Security Visibility into Confident Prioritization — Optional

Security teams already have ample visibility into vulnerabilities, but the real challenge is validating which exposures are actually exploitable and business-critical. BreachLock argues that Adversarial Exposure Validation helps turn overwhelming findings into confident remediation priorities by combining realistic attack validation with human expertise.

  • The article argues that security teams now have extensive visibility into their environments, but they struggle to determine which findings warrant action first.
  • The article states that organizations need to distinguish between theoretical exposure and practical, exploitable risk in order to prioritize remediation effectively.
  • Adversarial Exposure Validation is presented as a component of Continuous Threat Exposure Management that validates which exposures are reachable, exploitable, and consequential in an organization’s environment.
  • The article says AI can help with discovery, scale, signal processing, and analysis, but cannot independently solve prioritization decisions that require business context and human judgment.
  • The article concludes that confidence in prioritization is an operational security capability that helps teams communicate risk clearly and invest resources where they reduce the most exposure.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by software supply chain compromise, active exploitation of internet-facing web components, and financially motivated campaigns abusing trust signals and weak access controls. Reporting also highlights operational disruption across major collaboration and...

entry.title: Mastra npm Compromise, RoguePlanet CVE-2026-50656, and AI Tool Data Theft
entry.content: This digest is dominated by software supply chain and developer ecosystem risk, with multiple reports detailing compromised npm packages, malicious IDE plugins, and browser extensions harvesting AI-related data. The coverage also includes a publicly disclosed Microsoft Defender elevation-of-privilege zero-day and a confirmed Kodak data breach claimed by ShinyHunters. Multiple reports from Aikido, Socket, and The Hacker News describe a coordinated npm supply chain attack affecting more than 140 packages in the @mastra namespace. The malicious publications occurred on June 17, when a hijacked npm account belonging to a legitimate former Mastra contributor mass-published compromised versions across the scope within a short window. The affected packages reportedly did not embed malware directly, but instead added a dependency on easy-day-js, a malicious clone or typosquat of dayjs. The easy-day-js package used a postinstall hook to execute an obfuscated setup script during installation. That script downloaded a second-stage payload from attacker-controlled infrastructure, including 23[.]254[.]164[.]92:8000, wrote it to a randomly named JavaScript file in the operating system temporary directory, spawned it as a detached background process, and deleted itself. Socket reported that the loader also disabled TLS certificate validation before retrieving the payload. The recovered second-stage malware was described as a cross-platform Node.js tasking client with persistence mechanisms for Windows, macOS, and Linux. Its collection logic targeted host reconnaissance data, browser history from Chrome, Edge, and Brave, and more than 160 cryptocurrency wallet browser extensions. Researchers noted similarities to an earlier axios compromise, including the use of a malicious dependency, a clean decoy version preceding the malicious release, automatic postinstall execution, and self-deleting droppers. Aikido Security reported a coordinated malware campaign on the JetBrains Marketplace involving at least 15 malicious plugins masquerading as AI coding assistants. The plugins reportedly functioned as advertised while secretly exfiltrating AI provider API keys entered by users, including keys for OpenAI, SiliconFlow, and DeepSeek. The stolen keys were sent over plaintext HTTP to 39.107.60[.]51. Two of the reported JetBrains plugins, CodeGPT AI Assistant and DeepSeek AI Assist, were listed with more than 25,000 downloads each, although the authenticity of those counts was unclear. The campaign reflects continued attacker interest in developer environments where AI service credentials may be stored, entered, or reused across workflows. Separately, researcher Jean-Marie R. reported that two Chrome ad blocker extensions, Smart Adblocker and Adblock for Browser, captured users’ conversations with AI chatbots. The extensions reportedly provided genuine ad-blocking capability while operating an undisclosed telemetry channel that collected full AI conversation histories, model usage, and subscription tier metadata across services including ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Copilot, Perplexity, DeepSeek, Grok, and Meta AI. Microsoft confirmed it is working on a security update for a Microsoft Defender zero-day vulnerability publicly referred to as RoguePlanet and now tracked as CVE-2026-50656. The issue is described as an elevation-of-privilege flaw in the Microsoft Malware Protection Engine involving a race condition. Researcher Nightmare Eclipse published a proof-of-concept exploit in a self-hosted Git repository and claimed the exploit affects fully patched Windows 10 and Windows 11 systems. According to the reporting, the exploit can spawn command prompts with SYSTEM privileges and may work regardless of whether Defender real-time protection is enabled. The disclosure is also tied to an ongoing dispute between the researcher and Microsoft regarding bug bounty and vulnerability disclosure practices. Kodak confirmed that an unauthorized third party temporarily accessed a limited amount of company data. The company said it engaged external cybersecurity experts, is investigating what data was accessed and copied, and is working with law enforcement. Kodak also stated that it is confident there is no threat to its systems or operations. The ShinyHunters extortion group claimed responsibility for the incident on its dark web leak site. The group alleged it stole more than 2.2 million records containing customer personally identifiable information and internal corporate data, and threatened to leak the data on June 18, 2026.
entry.summary: This digest is dominated by software supply chain and developer ecosystem risk, with multiple reports detailing compromised npm packages, malicious IDE plugins, and browser extensions harvesting AI-related data. The coverage also includes a publicly disclosed Microsoft Defender...

entry.title: Mastra npm Supply Chain Attack Backdoors 140+ Packages via easy-day-js
entry.content: The coverage window is dominated by a significant npm supply chain compromise affecting the Mastra AI framework ecosystem. Reporting from StepSecurity describes a coordinated package manipulation incident in which a typosquatted dependency was introduced across more than 140 packages under the @mastra npm organization, exposing downstream development and CI/CD environments to a postinstall-based payload chain. The incident is notable for its scale, its targeting of an AI framework package namespace, and its use of a staged delivery mechanism designed to execute during package installation. The affected Mastra packages reportedly had combined weekly downloads exceeding 1.1 million, making this a high-impact software supply chain event for organizations consuming JavaScript and AI development dependencies. StepSecurity reported that on June 17, 2026, an attacker compromised the @mastra npm organization and added a dependency named easy-day-js across more than 140 Mastra packages. The package name closely resembled the legitimate dayjs library, indicating a typosquatting approach intended to blend into JavaScript dependency trees and exploit developer familiarity with common date/time libraries. The malicious package was staged across versions. Version 1.11.21 of easy-day-js was reportedly published as a clean bait package, while version 1.11.22 introduced an obfuscated postinstall dropper. This sequencing is consistent with supply chain tradecraft in which an attacker first establishes a benign-looking package history before pushing a malicious update after trust or dependency inclusion has been achieved. The reported malicious behavior centered on npm’s postinstall lifecycle hook. According to StepSecurity, the obfuscated script attempted to download a second-stage payload from https://23.254.164.92:8000/update/49890878, spawn it as a detached Node.js process, and then delete setup.cjs after execution. This design indicates an effort to move beyond package-level code execution into a more persistent or independently running payload while reducing local forensic artifacts tied to the installer script. The use of a direct IP address and nonstandard port for payload retrieval suggests attacker-controlled infrastructure rather than a commodity package registry-hosted payload. StepSecurity also reported that, during a controlled GitHub Actions test, its Harden Runner in block mode intercepted and blocked the outbound connection to 23.254.164.92:8000, preventing the stage-two payload from being retrieved in that environment. The reported scale of the compromised package set places this incident among high-concern npm ecosystem supply chain events, particularly because it affected an AI framework namespace with broad downstream usage. StepSecurity stated that the affected Mastra packages had more than 1.1 million combined weekly downloads, and assessed that anyone who installed an @mastra package on the day of the compromise should consider the environment compromised. For threat hunters and detection engineers, the reporting provides concrete infrastructure and behavioral details: the package name easy-day-js, malicious version 1.11.22, postinstall execution, retrieval from 23.254.164.92:8000, and execution of a detached Node.js second stage. The incident reinforces ongoing adversary interest in developer ecosystems, CI/CD workflows, and trusted open-source dependency chains as distribution mechanisms for code execution at scale.
entry.summary: The coverage window is dominated by a significant npm supply chain compromise affecting the Mastra AI framework ecosystem. Reporting from StepSecurity describes a coordinated package manipulation incident in which a typosquatted dependency was introduced across more than 140 packages under the...

entry.title: npm shai_hulululud Targets AI Malware Scanners With Prompt Injection
entry.content: The coverage window is dominated by a software supply chain finding involving an npm package that appears designed to interfere with AI-assisted malware analysis rather than deliver a conventional credential-stealing payload. Socket Threat Research reported that the package, shaihulululud@1.0.48596, uses prompt-injection-style content, context flooding, and layered obfuscation to complicate automated review. Socket Threat Research identified shaihulululud@1.0.48596 as a suspicious npm package that differs from recent Mini Shai-Hulud, Miasma, and Hades activity. Rather than carrying the credential theft behavior associated with those campaigns, the package appears aimed at disrupting AI-based malware scanners and analysis workflows. The package includes an approximately 9.28 MB index.js file containing policy-triggering prompt text, fake system override instructions, and tens of thousands of repeated comment lines. This material is not executed by JavaScript, but it can influence LLM-based scanners by consuming context windows, causing truncation, triggering refusals, or shifting attention away from executable logic. Socket also reported that executable JavaScript was appended after the large prompt and comment content. Researchers described the code as obfuscated through a character-code array, a ROT-style substitution function, eval usage, and a second AES-encrypted layer, and classified the package as protestware or potentially unwanted behavior. The report highlights a growing concern around adversarial content embedded in software packages to manipulate AI-enabled security tooling. In this case, the malicious or unwanted behavior is less about exploiting runtime environments directly and more about shaping how automated scanners interpret, summarize, or fail to analyze the package. The technique illustrates how non-executed source content can still have operational impact when analysis pipelines rely on large language models. Prompt-like instructions and repeated filler can create conditions where analysis output is incomplete, refused, or biased toward irrelevant text while executable code remains buried near the end of the file. This finding is notable for threat hunters, detection engineers, and SOC analysts tracking software supply chain abuse because it reflects a shift in attacker experimentation toward the analysis layer itself. The package demonstrates how npm ecosystem threats may increasingly combine traditional JavaScript obfuscation with content designed specifically to degrade AI-assisted triage and malware classification.
entry.summary: The coverage window is dominated by a software supply chain finding involving an npm package that appears designed to interfere with AI-assisted malware analysis rather than deliver a conventional credential-stealing payload. Socket Threat Research reported that the package,...

entry.title: JetBrains AI Key Theft, Vertex AI Bucket Squatting, Rokarolla, and REDCap Exposure
entry.content: This coverage window was dominated by software supply chain and developer-platform risk, especially where AI tooling intersects with credentials, model workflows, and autonomous agents. Reporting also highlighted continued abuse of trusted distribution channels, mobile banking malware, and exposed research infrastructure linked to espionage activity. Aikido Security and BleepingComputer reported a coordinated campaign involving at least 15 malicious JetBrains Marketplace plugins posing as AI coding assistants, code-review tools, and Git utilities. The plugins exfiltrated AI provider API keys entered into their settings to a hardcoded server at 39.107.60[.]51 over plain HTTP, while continuing to function as advertised. The plugins were published under seven vendor accounts and had nearly 70,000 reported installs, though Aikido noted that marketplace download counts and reviews may have been manipulated. The campaign reflects broader concern over developer machine compromise and package ecosystem abuse. StepSecurity described recent npm and PyPI attacks such as Miasma and Hades that install malicious package versions on developer workstations and harvest secrets, including registry publishing tokens. Its analysis focused on package-manager configuration files such as .npmrc, bunfig.toml, .yarnrc, .yarnrc.yml, and pip.conf as locations where effective registry settings and hardcoded credentials can expose developers and maintainers. A separate AI platform issue affected the Google Cloud Vertex AI SDK for Python, where predictable default staging bucket names enabled a bucket-squatting attack against model uploads. Unit 42 showed that an attacker with a victim project ID and region could pre-create the expected Cloud Storage bucket, receive uploaded model files, and replace them with a malicious pickle or joblib model before Vertex AI loaded it. Google addressed the issue by adding randomness to bucket names in version 1.144.0 and ownership verification in version 1.148.0. Netskope published analysis arguing that zero trust strategies are expanding beyond human users and workloads to include AI agents. The article cited Anthropic’s “Zero Trust for AI Agents” framing and emphasized agent identity as a control point for determining which agents can access which resources under specific conditions. The piece described blended identity models that combine an agent’s identity with the identity of the human invoking it, using contextual attributes such as time, location, sensitivity, risk score, and application instance. It also framed prompt injection, jailbreaking, anomalous behavior, and data exfiltration as security concerns for agent inputs and outputs. Zimperium reported a new Android banking trojan named Rokarolla targeting 217 banking and cryptocurrency applications with 137 supported commands. The malware is distributed through malicious websites impersonating Google Chrome or TikTok downloads, then presents itself as Google Play Protect during installation. Once launched, it requests Accessibility service access along with notification, SMS, and call permissions, and uses fake overlays to steal credentials, card data, and other financial information. Kaspersky also identified malware distribution through Steam Workshop content for the Wallpaper Engine application. Malicious “application wallpapers,” which are executable Windows applications, were downloaded thousands or tens of thousands of times before being reported. Observed payloads included DarkKomet, Lumma, Vidar, cryptocurrency miners, botnet loaders, RanEngine, and ransomware strains, with Steam removing the reported items after notification. Censys published an exposure analysis of REDCap following Google Threat Intelligence Group reporting on UNC6508, a PRC-nexus actor linked to a year-plus espionage campaign against North American academic, medical, and military research institutions. UNC6508 reportedly exploited an externally facing REDCap server, deployed a webshell, and used the INFINITERED PHP backdoor, although the initial access method remains unconfirmed. Censys observed just over 8,500 Internet-facing REDCap instances globally as of June 16, 2026, with 40% in the United States and limited adoption of the latest observed version. Health-ISAC separately reported that cyber insurance policyholders are facing heavier scrutiny in underwriting and claims despite declining rates. The article cited insurer concern over concentration in large U.S. policyholders and the potential for a major supply chain event or outage to create systemic impact. It also noted that organizations are examining continuity scenarios involving major IT outages caused by malicious activity or flawed updates.
entry.summary: This coverage window was dominated by software supply chain and developer-platform risk, especially where AI tooling intersects with credentials, model workflows, and autonomous agents. Reporting also highlighted continued abuse of trusted distribution channels, mobile banking malware, and exposed...

entry.title: CVE-2026-27577 n8n RCE, GhostTree Evasion, and AI Security Governance
entry.content: This digest window is dominated by software execution risk, endpoint evasion research, supply chain visibility, and the expanding governance burden around AI-enabled systems. Several reports also highlight how governments and platforms are turning to access restrictions and identity checks to address abuse, fraud, and child safety concerns, raising parallel security and privacy questions. CyCognito detailed CVE-2026-27577, a critical n8n code injection vulnerability that allows an authenticated user with workflow creation or editing permissions to execute system commands on the host through crafted workflow expressions. The flaw stems from a sandbox escape in n8n’s expression and code-node evaluation environment, involving PrototypeSanitizer behavior that can expose host process internals. Affected versions include n8n before 1.123.22, versions 2.0.0 through 2.9.2, and 2.10.0 across self-hosted and n8n Cloud deployments, with fixes released on February 25, 2026. Varonis described GhostTree, a Windows evasion technique that abuses recursive NTFS junctions to create effectively endless valid file path combinations. Because users with write access can create junctions without administrator privileges, GhostBranch and GhostTree structures can cause recursive scanners, including EDR products, to follow loops and fail to complete inspection of target folders. The report states that Microsoft later patched a Windows Defender bypass after initially closing the issue. Socket introduced Manifest Alerts to surface supply chain risk in project manifests, particularly missing lockfiles that leave dependency resolution unpinned. The feature was built after customer exposure analysis during the Axios npm compromise showed that risk was not limited to projects directly depending on the compromised Axios version. Socket separates these alerts from dependency alerts and reports the affected manifest, package-manager context, and impact details. Health-ISAC covered the FDA’s draft guidance for artificial intelligence-enabled medical devices, emphasizing how regulators are adapting to software that changes over time. The non-binding 2025 guidance calls out AI-specific risks including model drift, bias, and data poisoning, while expecting manufacturers to use predetermined change-control plans that preserve monitoring, testing, transparency, and auditability. Phil Englert of Health-ISAC framed the approach as recognition of AI’s evolving nature while maintaining accountability and patient safety. Censys described its integration with Google SecOps, positioning internet intelligence as enrichment for AI-enabled security operations workflows. The integration can add context about IPs, web properties, certificates, active DNS, historical observations, adversary clusters, and infrastructure relationships directly into SecOps cases and SOAR flows. The reporting emphasizes analyst pivoting from single indicators into related infrastructure and using historical lookups or rescans to understand how assets appeared during an incident window. NetSPI warned that regulated industries evaluating AI security tooling face practical constraints around determinism, auditability, trust boundaries, cost predictability, and evidentiary consistency. The article argues that many LLM-enabled security systems remain better suited as productivity enhancers for skilled practitioners than as replacements for human judgment. It also highlights budgeting uncertainty from token-based pricing and the potential mismatch between non-deterministic AI outputs and auditor or regulator expectations. The Record reported that India temporarily blocked Telegram nationwide ahead of the June 21 NEET-UG medical entrance exam rerun due to concerns the platform could be used for cheating. Authorities also directed Telegram to disable message editing in India until June 30, citing scams in which edited messages made fake leaked exam questions appear authentic after the fact. The National Testing Agency said cyber authorities removed many Telegram channels, groups, and bots advertising fraudulent exam paper access, while Ahmedabad police arrested members of a cyber fraud ring accused of operating eight Telegram channels and moving about 15 million rupees through fraudulent bank accounts. BleepingComputer reported that the UK plans to ban under-16s from social media, with regulations expected before Christmas and implementation anticipated in spring 2027. Covered platforms are expected to include user-to-user services with algorithmic feeds such as Instagram, YouTube, TikTok, Snapchat, Facebook, and X, while messaging services and YouTube Kids are excluded. New accounts may require age verification through identity document uploads or facial age scans, and experts cited in the article warned that these mechanisms can be bypassed while creating risks tied to biometric and identity data collection.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by software execution risk, endpoint evasion research, supply chain visibility, and the expanding governance burden around AI-enabled systems. Several reports also highlight how governments and platforms are turning to access restrictions and identity checks to...

entry.title: Rokarolla, FortiSandbox CVEs, DragonForce Teams Relays, and LiteSpeed cPanel Exploitation
entry.content: Reporting in this window centered on active exploitation of exposed infrastructure, increasingly evasive malware tradecraft, and the continuing shift toward attack-path-based exposure management. Several items highlighted how attackers are blending legitimate services, anonymizing infrastructure, and trusted communication channels into intrusion workflows, complicating detection and attribution. Mobile banking malware, ransomware operations, phishing, cloud audit fidelity, and exploited appliance flaws all featured prominently. The coverage also reflected growing attention to continuous controls validation, operational resilience in healthcare, and AI-enabled analysis in OT environments. CISA added an actively exploited LiteSpeed cPanel user-end plugin vulnerability, identified in the reporting as CVE-2026-48172, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog. The flaw affects LiteSpeed cPanel user-end plugin versions before 2.4.8 and reportedly allows attackers with FTP or web shell access to escalate privileges to root on shared hosting servers running CloudLinux/CageFS. CISA ordered U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to secure affected systems within three days under BOD 26-04 guidance. Fortinet FortiSandbox also appeared in exploitation reporting, with Defused Cyber observing activity against CVE-2026-39813, CVE-2026-39808, and CVE-2026-25089. The first two issues involve JRPC API path traversal and OS command injection, both potentially reachable through crafted HTTP requests by unauthenticated attackers. CVE-2026-25089 was patched more recently, and Defused Cyber assessed that the observed exploit attempt for that flaw appeared AI-generated and faulty, with no working public exploit known. Zimperium documented Rokarolla, a new Android banking trojan targeting 217 banking and cryptocurrency apps with support for 137 remote commands. The malware is distributed through malicious sites impersonating popular applications such as TikTok and Chrome, with an initial dropper posing as Google Play Protect. Once installed, it abuses Accessibility permissions to disable protections, capture lock-screen credentials, deploy fake login overlays, read and send SMS messages, log screens and keystrokes, and silently replace copied cryptocurrency wallet addresses. DragonForce ransomware was reported abusing Microsoft Teams TURN relay infrastructure through a custom Go-based backdoor named Backdoor.Turn. Symantec described it as the first known in-the-wild malware to use Teams relay servers to mask command-and-control traffic. In a December 2025 intrusion against a major U.S. services company, the attackers likely gained access through an unknown SQL or MSSQL server flaw, used BYOVD techniques with multiple vulnerable drivers to obtain kernel-level privileges, exfiltrated data, and deployed ransomware. Business email compromise reporting from IRONSCALES showed a same-day payment request sent from a legitimate construction consulting domain via hosted Exchange infrastructure. SPF passed and DMARC returned bestguesspass, while the body fabricated a prior thread using real employee names and attached a clean PDF invoice with Wells Fargo banking details. The x-originating-ip header pointed to 45[.]67[.]97[.]6 in Seoul, inconsistent with the Albuquerque-based sender, and the message was mitigated before recipient action. A Spur Intelligence survey of more than 200 practitioners found anonymizing infrastructure, including VPNs and residential proxy networks, present in nearly every security incident. Nearly half of surveyed organizations reported significant operational or financial impact from account takeover attempts and credential abuse conducted through these services. Respondents also cited lack of IP context as a major analysis challenge, with many teams still using IP intelligence primarily after alerts are generated. Permiso highlighted risks around GCP’s deprecated serviceData field and the broader dependency of cloud detection engineering on audit log quality. The article emphasized that administrative activity in GCP is represented through audit log entries and that their structure and completeness directly affect detection rules, investigations, and post-incident reconstruction. The reporting framed log fidelity as a core constraint on reliable cloud threat detection. Tenable and XM Cyber coverage reflected the continued movement from traditional vulnerability counting toward continuous threat exposure management and attack-path prioritization. Tenable One’s continuous control validation maps defenses such as EDR, MFA, firewalls, SIEM, and DLP onto potential attack paths, while XM Cyber’s SC Awards Europe win was tied to attack simulation, attack graph analysis, and identification of choke points where a single remediation can disrupt multiple routes to critical assets. Radiflow described AI’s dual role in OT security: a force multiplier for attackers through deepfakes, automated phishing, faster scanning, and vulnerability chaining, and a defensive aid for asset visibility, threat intelligence, prioritization, and SOC efficiency. Its reporting focused on passive OT network learning through iSID and digital-twin-based breach-and-attack simulation through CIARA. In healthcare, Rady Children’s Hospital-San Diego CISO Sahan Fernando emphasized operational engagement as central to cyber resilience and continuity of patient care, citing sector-wide collaboration through Health-ISAC, the Health Sector Coordinating Council, and Operation Vital Signs.
entry.summary: Reporting in this window centered on active exploitation of exposed infrastructure, increasingly evasive malware tradecraft, and the continuing shift toward attack-path-based exposure management. Several items highlighted how attackers are blending legitimate services, anonymizing infrastructure,...
